title: Groups & Teams
simple_html:
Most real work happens in groups. A group is two or more people who interact toward a shared goal; a team goes further — members combine their skills to produce something none could achieve alone, and they hold each other accountable. Understanding how teams form, behave, and sometimes fail is essential for any manager.

How teams develop

Tuckman's famous model says teams pass through five stages: Forming (polite, uncertain), Storming (conflict over roles), Norming (settling into shared rules), Performing (productive), and Adjourning (wrapping up). Conflict in the storming stage is normal, not a failure.

When teams go wrong

  • Social loafing — people exert less effort in a group because their contribution is hidden.
  • Groupthink — a desire for harmony leads members to suppress doubts and make bad decisions.

What makes teams work

Effective teams have clear goals, the right mix of roles, mutual trust, manageable size, and individual accountability. Diversity of skills helps, but only if conflict stays focused on the task, not on personalities.

Key takeaways

  • Teams differ from groups by shared accountability and combined skills.
  • Teams develop through predictable stages; storming conflict is normal.
  • Social loafing and groupthink are the two classic team failures.
  • Accountability, trust, clear goals, and the right size make teams effective.
intermediate_html:
Organizations increasingly organize work around teams, making this a core OB topic. A group is two or more interdependent individuals who interact to achieve objectives; a team generates positive synergy through coordinated effort, so its output exceeds the sum of individual inputs and members share mutual accountability. This lesson covers team types, development stages, productivity threats, roles, and the drivers of effectiveness.

Types of teams

  • Problem-solving teams — share ideas to improve processes.
  • Self-managed teams — take on supervisory responsibilities.
  • Cross-functional teams — members from different areas tackle a shared task.
  • Virtual teams — collaborate across distance via technology.

Stages of team development

Tuckman's (1965) model describes five stages: Forming (orientation, uncertainty), Storming (conflict over roles and influence), Norming (cohesion and shared norms develop), Performing (the team works effectively), and Adjourning (disbanding). An important alternative, Gersick's (1988) punctuated equilibrium model, found that project teams do little until a midpoint "wake-up call" triggers a burst of focused activity — challenging the idea of smooth linear progression.

Threats to team productivity

Social loafing (Latané, Williams & Harkins, 1979) is the tendency to exert less effort collectively than individually, because individual contributions are unidentifiable and accountability is diffused. Remedies: smaller teams, individual accountability, measurable contributions, and meaningful tasks. Groupthink (Janis, 1972) is the deterioration of decision quality when a cohesive group's desire for unanimity overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives; symptoms include illusions of invulnerability, self-censorship, and pressure on dissenters. Remedies include encouraging dissent, assigning a devil's advocate, and bringing in outside views.

Team roles and conflict

Belbin's (1981) team roles framework argues balanced teams need a mix of nine role types (e.g., Plant for ideas, Coordinator, Implementer, Completer-Finisher, Monitor-Evaluator). A vital distinction is task conflict vs. relationship conflict (Jehn, 1995): moderate task conflict (about ideas) can improve decisions, while relationship conflict (interpersonal friction) almost always harms performance.

Worked example: a stalled product team

A six-person cross-functional team misses milestones. Diagnosis: it is stuck in storming — engineering and marketing clash over priorities (relationship conflict, not productive task conflict). Two quiet members show social loafing because tasks are assigned to "the team," not individuals. The lead, fearing discord, suppresses debate — early groupthink. Interventions: clarify individual ownership of deliverables (cuts loafing), reframe disputes around shared goals and data to convert relationship conflict into task conflict, appoint a devil's advocate, and use Belbin to check the team has a Completer-Finisher to drive closure. The team moves toward norming and performing.

How to apply it

  1. Diagnose the development stage before intervening; expect and normalize storming.
  2. Design out social loafing with individual accountability and the right (small) size.
  3. Encourage task conflict while actively managing relationship conflict.
  4. Balance team roles and guard against groupthink with structured dissent.
full_html:
Contemporary organizations rely heavily on teams, making group dynamics central to organizational behavior. The distinction is foundational: a group is two or more interacting, interdependent individuals pursuing particular objectives, whereas a team coordinates effort to produce positive synergy, such that collective performance exceeds the sum of individual contributions, with members holding shared (not merely individual) accountability (Katzenbach & Smith, 1993). Not every group is a team, and converting groups into teams requires deliberate design.

Types and inputs of teams

Common forms include problem-solving, self-managed, cross-functional, and virtual teams. Effective teams can be analyzed through an input-process-output lens (Hackman, 1987): inputs (context such as resources, leadership, and reward structure; composition such as ability, personality, and roles; and appropriate size) shape processes (a shared mental model, common purpose, specific goals, team efficacy, and managed conflict) that yield outputs (performance and member satisfaction). Research consistently finds that smaller teams (often five to nine members) coordinate better and suffer less free-riding, and that diversity is a double-edged sword — informational diversity can improve creativity and decision quality, while surface-level diversity can initially impede cohesion.

Models of team development

The canonical model is Tuckman's (1965; Tuckman & Jensen, 1977) five-stage sequence: Forming (orientation and dependence, with members testing boundaries), Storming (intragroup conflict over roles, status, and approach), Norming (development of cohesion, shared norms, and identity), Performing (energy channeled into the task with effective, flexible role structures), and Adjourning (disengagement and closure for temporary teams). A major qualification comes from Gersick's (1988) punctuated equilibrium model: studying real project groups, she found teams establish a pattern early, then make little progress until a midpoint transition — roughly halfway to the deadline — triggers a burst of reorientation and accelerated work. This implies that calendar pressure, not orderly maturation, often drives output, and that leaders should leverage the midpoint as a natural intervention point.

Process losses: social loafing and groupthink

Two seminal phenomena explain why teams can underperform their potential (Steiner, 1972, called the gap "process loss"). Social loafing — empirically established by Latané, Williams and Harkins (1979) building on Ringelmann's rope-pulling observations — is the systematic reduction of individual effort as group size grows, driven by diffusion of responsibility and the unidentifiability of contributions. It is mitigated by establishing individual accountability, keeping teams small, measuring individual contributions, selecting motivated members, and making tasks meaningful. Groupthink (Janis, 1972, 1982) is a mode of thinking in highly cohesive, insulated groups under directive leadership and stress, in which the drive for concurrence overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives. Its symptoms — illusions of invulnerability and unanimity, collective rationalization, stereotyping of out-groups, self-censorship, mindguards, and pressure on dissenters — produce defective decisions (Janis's cases included the Bay of Pigs). Countermeasures include encouraging open dissent, appointing a devil's advocate, soliciting outside expertise, leader impartiality, and second-chance meetings. A related risk is group polarization, the tendency for group discussion to amplify members' initial leanings.

Roles, norms, and conflict

Belbin's (1981) team roles research argued that high-performing teams need a balance of nine behavioral roles — for example Plant (creative ideas), Resource Investigator, Coordinator, Shaper, Monitor-Evaluator, Teamworker, Implementer, Completer-Finisher, and Specialist — and that role gaps (e.g., no Completer-Finisher) predict failure. Norms — shared expectations about behavior — powerfully shape conduct, as the Hawthorne studies first revealed. On conflict, Jehn's (1995) crucial distinction separates task conflict (disagreement about content and ideas), which at moderate levels can enhance decision quality in non-routine work, from relationship conflict (interpersonal incompatibility and emotional friction) and process conflict (disputes over logistics), both of which generally harm performance and satisfaction. The managerial art is to stimulate functional task conflict while preventing its degeneration into relationship conflict (Amason, 1996). Team cohesion aids performance mainly when group norms support high performance; cohesive teams with low-performance norms underperform.

Worked example: rescuing a virtual product team

An eight-person cross-functional, partly virtual team is behind schedule near its midpoint. Diagnosis using the frameworks: the team is mired in storming, with engineering-marketing disagreements that have shifted from ideas to personal blame (relationship conflict); two remote members exhibit social loafing because deliverables are assigned to "the team"; and the lead's push for quick consensus shows early groupthink. Gersick's model suggests the midpoint is the ideal moment to intervene. Actions: assign individually owned, measurable deliverables (reducing loafing); recharter around a common goal and use data to convert relationship conflict back into task conflict; appoint a devil's advocate and invite an outside reviewer (countering groupthink); apply Belbin to confirm a Completer-Finisher and Coordinator exist; and strengthen virtual-team norms (response-time expectations, synchronous check-ins, richer channels). The team transitions to norming and performing.

Nuances, debates, and common mistakes

  • Linear stages oversimplify. Tuckman's model is a useful heuristic, but Gersick shows progress is often discontinuous; teams may revisit stages.
  • Cohesion is not always good. It boosts performance only with high-performance norms and can fuel groupthink.
  • Treating all conflict as harmful. Suppressing task conflict can entrench groupthink; the goal is to channel it.
  • Ignoring free-riding. Assigning work to "the group" without individual accountability invites social loafing.
  • Over-teaming. Not every task needs a team; teams add coordination cost and can dilute accountability.

Cross-links

This lesson builds on Individual Differences (Lesson 2), since personality and roles drive team composition, and on Motivation (Lessons 3-4), since accountability and meaningful tasks counter loafing. It feeds directly into Communication (Lesson 6) — especially channel richness for virtual teams — and into Leadership (Lesson 7) and Power, Politics & Conflict (Lesson 8), where conflict-handling styles and negotiation are developed. Team norms also foreshadow Organizational Culture (Lesson 9).
